title: User units and network-online.target
body:
### Issue Description

When you try to start a user container using quadlet it by default depends on the unit `podman-user-wait-network-online.service` which in turn runs `until systemctl is-active network-online.target; do sleep 0.5; done`. The problem is that (at least on Arch Linux) the `network-online.target` unit is not active and therefore the `podman-user-wait-network-online.service` unit always fails.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Configure a container unit in `.config/containers/systemd/`, for example:
   ```
   [Unit]
   Description=Ollama Open WebUI container

   [Container]
   Image=ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:ollama
   Pull=newer
   PublishPort=127.0.0.1:8090:8080
   PublishPort=127.0.0.1:11434:11434
   Environment=WEBUI_AUTH=false
   Environment=OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0:11434
   PodmanArgs=--gpus=all

   [Service]
   Restart=always
   TimeoutStartSec=900
   ```
2. Start it up: `systemctl --user start ollama`
3. Check the journal `journalctl --user -e`
   ```
   dic 08 13:24:56 hell systemd[1601]: Starting Wait for system level network-online.target as user....
   dic 08 13:24:56 hell sh[3128]: inactive
   dic 08 13:24:56 hell sh[3130]: inactive
   dic 08 13:24:57 hell sh[3132]: inactive
   dic 08 13:24:57 hell sh[3134]: inactive
   dic 08 13:24:58 hell sh[3136]: inactive
   ...
   ...
   dic 08 13:26:25 hell sh[3604]: inactive
   dic 08 13:26:25 hell sh[3606]: inactive
   dic 08 13:26:26 hell systemd[1601]: podman-user-wait-network-online.service: start operation timed out. Terminating.
   dic 08 13:26:26 hell systemd[1601]: podman-user-wait-network-online.service: Main process exited, code=killed, status=15/TERM
   dic 08 13:26:26 hell systemd[1601]: podman-user-wait-network-online.service: Failed with result 'timeout'.
   dic 08 13:26:26 hell systemd[1601]: Failed to start Wait for system level network-online.target as user..
   ```


### Describe the results you received

The system fails when trying to determine the network status. This happens because the `network-online.target` is not actually active:
```
% systemctl status network-online.target 
○ network-online.target - Network is Online
     Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/network-online.target; static)
     Active: inactive (dead)
       Docs: man:systemd.special(7)
             https://systemd.io/NETWORK_ONLINE
```

This however does not affect other units that depend on this target, for example with this user unit in `.config/systemd/user/test.service`:
```systemd
[Unit]
Description=test unit
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target

[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/bin/echo hello
```

If we start it with `systemctl --user start test.service` it starts without problems.

### Describe the results you expected

There should be another way to determine if the network online target has been reached for the user. As far as I see systemd does it, but I don't know how.

### podman info output

```yaml

  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.38.0
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

It is a fully upgraded Arch Linux system.

### Additional information

In the configuration of the container unit I have tried to put the following so that at least it would not try to execute the `podman-user-wait-network-online.service`.
```
[Quadlet]
DefaultDependencies=false
```

I don't know why but it still executes this dependency, maybe because my unit depends on some volumes?

comments.body:
https://systemd.io/NETWORK_ONLINE
I think it not a bug
`systemctl status network-online.target`
If you're using systemd, then you'll definitely have this target, it's just that it makes a difference when this target is reached, depending on what network manager you're using

The targets and units of systemd are categorized into system and user, and network-online.target does not exist in user by default, which means that if you fill `network-online.target` in the user-unit in this way, it won't actually do anything.
If you're using `NetworkManager` or `systemd-networkd`, that shouldn't be a problem

The truth is that I thought it could be a bug in Arch. I'm using `NetworkManager` on my machine, but I've set up a virtual machine with clean Arch Linux using `systemd-networkd` and the exact same thing happens:
```
[vagrant@archlinux ~]$ systemctl status systemd-networkd
● systemd-networkd.service - Network Configuration
     Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/systemd-networkd.service; enabled; preset: enabled)
     Active: active (running) since Mon 2024-12-09 08:58:44 UTC; 1min 11s ago
 Invocation: a0b0da8a6f76411289d70f8303bf65dc
TriggeredBy: ● systemd-networkd.socket
       Docs: man:systemd-networkd.service(8)
             man:org.freedesktop.network1(5)
   Main PID: 283 (systemd-network)
     Status: "Processing requests..."
      Tasks: 1 (limit: 530)
   FD Store: 0 (limit: 512)
     Memory: 3.4M (peak: 3.7M)
        CPU: 22ms
     CGroup: /system.slice/systemd-networkd.service
             └─283 /usr/lib/systemd/systemd-networkd

Warning: some journal files were not opened due to insufficient permissions.
```
```
[vagrant@archlinux ~]$ systemctl status network-online.target
○ network-online.target - Network is Online
     Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/network-online.target; static)
     Active: inactive (dead)
       Docs: man:systemd.special(7)
             https://systemd.io/NETWORK_ONLINE
```

</details>
Did you enable `NetworkManager-wait-online.service` ?

> Did you enable `NetworkManager-wait-online.service` ?

Yes:
```
% systemctl status NetworkManager-wait-online.service
○ NetworkManager-wait-online.service - Network Manager Wait Online
     Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/NetworkManager-wait-online.service; enabled; preset: disabled)
     Active: inactive (dead)
       Docs: man:NetworkManager-wait-online.service(8)
```

What is the output of `systemctl list-dependencies network-online.target`? The target unit itself is not so important and does not need to be enabled. It just acts as single unit where other units from the network management tool hook into, i.e.  `NetworkManager-wait-online.service` in case of NetworkManager.

In my system where I use `NetworkManager`:
```
% systemctl list-dependencies network-online.target
network-online.target
○ └─NetworkManager-wait-online.service
```

In a virtual machine with a clean system using `systemd-networkd`:
```
$ systemctl list-dependencies network-online.target
network-online.target
○ └─systemd-networkd-wait-online.service
```

In both cases the dependent service is enabled and inactive.

Ok that is odd but this all is far outside of the control of podman I believe.

Can you check `systemctl list-dependencies --reverse  network-online.target`? I believe the enable command for `NetworkManager-wait-online.service` doesn't actually start the unit at boot by default, it just causes it to get triggered by `network-online.target` because of

```
...
[Install]
WantedBy=network-online.target
```

And `network-online.target` doesn't start on its own either, it only get run when other units declare:
```
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target
```

So I think if you have no other root service starting it it might never activate.


I really wish we would not need to be in the business of that stuff but here we are until https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/3312 is fixed. We need a work around it as rootless containers start without networking otherwise https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22197

```
% systemctl list-dependencies --reverse network-online.target
network-online.target
○ └─archlinux-keyring-wkd-sync.service
```

`archlinux-keyring-wkd-sync.service` is a service that runs through a timer once a week.

Therefore, as you say, there is no other (system) service that activates this target and therefore it remains inactive.

So we may need a dummy system level service requires this target
Can you try it？

A dummy unit might not be required, enabling a service is just adding a symlink, adding a link like
`/etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/network-online.target -> /usr/lib/systemd/system/network-online.target` might be enough. That way the unit will get started when `multi-user.target` is reached which should always be there.

Creating a service like this and enabling it works:
```
# /etc/systemd/system/podman-network-online-dummy.service
[Unit]
Description=This service simply activates network-online.target
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
ExecStart=/usr/bin/echo Activating network-online.target

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

```
$ systemctl status podman-network-online-dummy
● podman-network-online-dummy.service - This service simply activates network-online.target
     Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/podman-network-online-dummy.service; enabled; preset: disabled)
     Active: inactive (dead) since Mon 2024-12-09 12:37:14 UTC; 2min 14s ago
   Duration: 13ms
 Invocation: 2badd17dfc034d7098894fe20496afab
    Process: 573 ExecStart=/usr/bin/echo Activating network-online.target (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)
   Main PID: 573 (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)
   Mem peak: 1.6M
        CPU: 3ms


$ systemctl status network-online.target
● network-online.target - Network is Online
     Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/network-online.target; static)
     Active: active since Mon 2024-12-09 12:37:14 UTC; 30s ago
 Invocation: cb64d12a239a4782a5fa3dc88e7cf750
       Docs: man:systemd.special(7)
             https://systemd.io/NETWORK_ONLINE
```

I have a similar problem running my user qualets in fedora because the **podman-user-wait-network-online.service** timesout.

What I have noticed, is this service times out for me because, I have my systemctl overridden by a function to default systemctl with the --user flag instead of --system one.

So, in my case the service is run as systemctl --user session which does not recognize  **network-online.target** from the --system session, which causes the timeouts.  

  **ExecStart=sh -c 'until systemctl is-active network-online.target; do sleep 0.5; done'** runs in the users session, as well as the users terminal, which picks up the function's version of systemctl command.

I believe upstream should specify the absolute path of this unit to prevent session cross contamination. But since I don't know if that's intentional or not, and because I overrode the command, I corrected my timeouts by using a drop-in override.conf to set the absolute path of systemctl.
FWIW, this affects  openSUSE MicroOS as well.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I hate lazy use of github-actions to close issues instead of actually fixing them.

@udf2457 First of all the issue is not closed as you can see. And we do not use the bot to auto close issues on our repos so please do not complain about that.

Second this is a FOSS project and we accept contributions. So if you like to get this fixed the best way is to make a PR with a fix.
Because frankly I am out of ideas here, there is no easy way forward. We cannot just active network-online.service as root... (because well we run as rootless). And even if we were to ship a unit that is enabled by default as root to trigger the network-online.target I am sure we get yelled at quickly by many distro maintainers that we should not do that (which I agree with).  

As pasta behaves better now reverting might be an option but then again I am not sure what else we might break with that. The best way way would be to have this fixed in systemd of course.

I completely agree with what you say @Luap99 there is no solution to the problem beyond packaging a dummy unit that simply has `network-online.target` as a dependency and the user enables it in his system. This is a workaround and it is not ideal at all (besides that possibly all distributions would oppose it as you say).

From my point of view I agree with you that the solution should come from systemd and that is where the focus should be.
Probably worth you commenting here @Luap99 @ogarcia https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/28762

@Luap99 Thank you for clarifying you do not use the stale-bot to autoclose, sadly the majority of GH repos who use stale-bot do autoclose, hence you can't blame me for assuming you were just following the herd on brushing things under the carpet.

Getting things back on-topic I hope you can contribute at least a few comments to the `systemd/systemd` issue I highlighted above.
There is already https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/3312 as I linked above and I commented there already. I have no influence over systemd whatsoever so I am not sure what you expect from me.
Just for the record, this problem also exists when using quadlets in Fedora CoreOS 41 (not in 40)
I ran into a similar issue on Flatcar Linux affecting user units.  This is how I fixed it: https://github.com/flatcar/Flatcar/issues/1666#issuecomment-2695537739

It 'works' for me by first running `sudo systemctl restart network-online.target` before running any containers.

Hopefully it can be fixed soon, or did I miss something?

I ran into a similar issue with podman machine on MacOS but none of the workarounds here work. So the irony is that the fancy new recommended quadlets don't work, but what works is the deprecated command that generates  systemd units. So that is the workaround i'm rolling with.

I also have this issue on openSUSE MicroOS, the dummy unit workaround fixed the issue for me. for the longest time I just assumed some part of podman was painfully slow, I'm glad to know that's not the case but there really should be a fix or workaround for this that's packaged with podman (possibly modifying `podman-user-wait-network-online.service`?), or at the very least, some sort of warning message the podman CLI prints if it detects this happening.

> [@Merlin04](https://github.com/Merlin04) I actually think it's a openSUSE issue, I don't have this issues on Fedora.

Also an Archlinux issue..
Encountered now on debian trixie as well
Fedora CoreOS (42.20250818.3.0) also has this problem.
I’ve just submitted a bug report to openSUSE regarding this issue. Thank you @ogarcia, for providing a workaround.

I had this issue on NixOS (unstable rev: c23193b943c6c689d70ee98ce3128239ed9e32d1) using podman from home-manager.

I didn't applied the fix suggest, instead I went with this:

`systemd.targets.network-online.wantedBy = [ "multi-user.target" ];`

After reading of the docs (https://wiki.nixos.org/wiki/Systemd/networkd) and looking at the options under `systemd.network` (in specific `systemd.network.wait-online.enable` and `systemd.network.wait-online.anyInterface` ) I understood that (maybe I got it wrong) that the options only enable the `network-online.target` and `systemd-networkd-wait-online.service` but they don't activate it, the way to achieve this accordingly do the NixOS  is to set `[ "multi-user.target" ]` for that `systemd.targets` (https://search.nixos.org/options?channel=unstable&show=systemd.targets.%3Cname%3E.enable&query=systemd.targets)

I would say overall we should likely revert the work around, maybe as part of 6.0. pasta has a "local mode" now so it should startup fine before the network is ready though there are still some problem with it since the addresses are not updated so we likely would want to wait for the netlink monitor feature of pasta as described here https://pad.passt.top/p/PastaWithoutNetworkConnectivity if it will be implemented.

cc @sbrivio-rh

You have "dev" in your nickname, so, while somebody else is already working on this (https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=141), it's a task that can definitely be split.

@ogarcia @richiedaze @findesgh @quaintdev My mind briefly ventured back to this problem, I have not tested as am busy with $work but do you think changing the Podman shipped`podman-user-wait-network-online.service` to something like:

```
[Unit]
Description=Wait for system level network-online.target as user.
Documentation=https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/22197
Documentation=man:podman-systemd.unit(5)
# Note in this instance using network.target not network-online.target 
# because we still need the network stack to be up to do the checks
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
# Set a timeout as by default oneshot does not have one and in case network-online.target
# never comes online we do not want to block forever, 90s is the default systemd unit timeout.
TimeoutStartSec=90s
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c '\
    for i in $(seq 1 120); do \
        # 1. Existing network-online.target check \
        if systemctl is-active -q network-online.target; then exit 0; fi; \
        # 2. Fallback: Check for configured IP address \
        # 'scope global' allows for air-gapped LANs \
        # where no default gateway is set on the machine
        if ip -o addr show scope global | grep -q .; then exit 0; fi; \
        sleep 0.5; \
    done; \
    echo "Timeout waiting for network"; exit 1'
RemainAfterExit=yes
```

I guess the problem is that not all systems have `iproute2` installed by default. Perhaps something under `/proc/net` could help.

> Maybe Fedora does patch the systemd service?

I think it's just that some service in Fedora depends on `network-online.target`, causing it to become active. As noted elsewhere in the thread (at least to my memory) if nothing depends on the target, it might be enabled but will never become active. That's why the dummy service workaround exists.

> > if ip -o addr show scope global | grep -q .; then exit 0; fi; \
> 
> I guess the problem is that not all systems have `iproute2` installed by default. Perhaps something under `/proc/net` could help.

We're working on fixing that properly as part of https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=141 / https://pad.passt.top/p/netlinkMonitor but I feel your pain meanwhile so here are some gross hacks with POSIX shell and utilities only, I hope this helps:

```
[ -n "$(head -n-2 /proc/net/route)" ] && echo ready
```
or
```
r=$(cat /proc/net/route); [ ${#r} -gt 127 ] && echo ready
```

You can test this with stand-alone pasta(1) for convenience, as it runs your command in an unconfigured network namespace (by default, without `--config-net`):
```
$ [ -n "$(head -n-2 /proc/net/route)" ] && echo ready
ready
$ pasta -- sh -c '[ -n "$(head -n-2 /proc/net/route)" ] && echo ready'
$
```

Would it be possible to add a tiny `-q` parameter to the podman-user-wait-network-online.service ?

Due to a systemd limitation, user units cannot depend on system-level units.

Since no system-level service depends on ```network-online.target```, it won’t be started even if the network is already connected.

Just a workaround: create a service that depends on network-online.target, e.g., ```/etc/systemd/system/custom-check-network-online-anchor.service```

```ini
[Unit]
Description=Custom Anchor to Force Network-Online Target
Wants=network-online.target
After=network-online.target
[Service]
Type=oneshot
RemainAfterExit=yes
ExecStart=/usr/bin/true
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

This is such a sadness 😭  and it's been years.
Installed fresh Debian 13 (trixie) and openSUSE Leap 16.0 and both still have this issue that `network-online.target` never becomes active causing `podman-user-wait-network-online.service` to fail after a long while.

I think the best workaround is simply doing:
```
$ ln -sf /usr/lib/systemd/system/network-online.target /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/network-online.target
```
This will make that `network-online.target` will become active. I think distributions should ship this because otherwise stuff is just broken.
Sadly can't do `systemctl enable network-online.target` because it doesn't have `WantedBy`

Shorter version
```sh
systemctl --system add-requires network.target network-online.target
```
Created symlink '/etc/systemd/system/network.target.requires/network-online.target' → '/usr/lib/systemd/system/network-online.target'.

This edit fixes **network-online.target** to start and stop when **network.target** does, correcting this issue. I have tested this edit and confirm it worked for me. Please test this edit to verify it fixes this issue so that I can report it upstream. Ty.

Also, @Luap99 Can we hard code the **--system** flag in **podman-user-wait-network-online.service** to prevent the issue I explained above?

They are different targets with different meanings unfortunately.

https://systemd.io/NETWORK_ONLINE/

I solve this with masking `podman-user-wait-network-online.service` and linking `network-online.target` to my user.

```bash
systemctl --user mask podman-user-wait-network-online.service
systemctl --user link $(systemctl show --property FragmentPath network-online.target | cut -d "=" -f 2)
```

> They are different targets with different meanings unfortunately.
> 
> https://systemd.io/NETWORK_ONLINE/
### Network connectivity has been established: network-online.target
network-online.target is a target that actively waits until the network is “up”, where the definition of “up” is defined by the network management software.

**network-online.target is never activated on GNOME.**

> I solve this with masking `podman-user-wait-network-online.service` and linking `network-online.target` to my user.
> 
> systemctl --user mask podman-user-wait-network-online.service
> systemctl --user link $(systemctl show --property FragmentPath network-online.target | cut -d "=" -f 2)

I had a similar issue on Debian 13 while setting up and debugging Ceph. The containers didn't get the DNS after rebooting the machine, as shown below.

```
~ for cnt in $(podman ps -q); do  podman exec -it $cnt /bin/sh -c 'cat /etc/resolv.conf'; done
~
```

 They would only get it after i manually restarted each container via Podman.

```
~ for cnt in $(podman ps -q); do  podman container restart $cnt; done
aaaaaaaaaaaa
bbbbbbbbbbbb
cccccccccccc
dddddddddddd
~ for cnt in $(podman ps -q); do  podman exec -it $cnt /bin/sh -c 'cat /etc/resolv.conf'; done
nameserver 10.10.1.21
...
```

Despite container services containing the `After=network-online.target` configuration.

```
~ grep -e After -e Before /etc/systemd/system/ceph-abc...df@.service 
After=network-online.target local-fs.target time-sync.target
Before=ceph-abc...df.target
```
 
To fix this I enabled the `ifupdown-wait-online.service` service. Then changed a configuration file of it's source script.

```
~ cat /etc/default/networking
...
# - none: don't wait.
WAIT_ONLINE_METHOD=route
# Which interface to wait for
...
```
I uncommented the `WAIT_ONLINE_METHOD` line and changed it's value to `route` as the default `ifup` wasn't working.

After that it was smooth sailing and all of the containers got thier DNS after the machine rebooted.

```
~ reboot
...
~ for cnt in $(podman ps -q); do  podman exec -it $cnt /bin/sh -c 'cat /etc/resolv.conf'; done
nameserver 10.10.1.21
...
```

If my understanding of the issue is right, any rootless podman container that needs outbound network connection would fail because of this bug. And podman loves to talk about the feature that unlike docker, it can run rootlessly.

Maybe the fundamental problem here is `until systemctl is-active network-online.target; do sleep 0.5; done` in `podman-user-wait-network-online.service`.

Maybe the `podman` maintainers need to replace it with something a bit more reliable, perhaps
`until ip route show | grep -q via; do sleep 0.5; done` ?

@Luap99
@GreatBigWhiteWorld Not 100% sure but I think Debian explicitly transitions the network to an "online" state before finishing the boot sequence.

> Maybe the podman maintainers need to replace it with something a bit more reliable, perhaps
until ip route show | grep -q via; do sleep 0.5; done ?

The problem is that also make assumptions, technically there is no requirement to have a route with `via` and this also only checks for ipv4 routes. There are ipv6 only systems as well albeit much rather of course.
If people have better commands to check sure we can discus them but the nice thing about the network-online.target thing is removes us from guessing what online means on each system.

Anyhow as I and @sbrivio-rh already mentioned the proper fix is in pasta and make it dynamically update the ip addresses
https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/issues/24796#issuecomment-3410969252
https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/issues/24796#issuecomment-3821682627

And with that we could then drop this ugly work around.

> Isn't redhat the backer of podman project? How come debian works better?

So? Better in what way? Because of one bug which heavily depends on system configuration which vastly differs between each user debain is somehow better? There are enough bugs that will only affect debian or another distro, not all bugs will be encounter on all distros. So this does not make one distro fundamentally better than another.

> If my understanding of the issue is right, any rootless podman container that needs outbound network connection would fail because of this bug. And podman loves to talk about the feature that unlike docker, it can run rootlessly.

No not at all, the issue as described in the original report that if network-online.target is not activated in the systemd system session that we loop 90s until we hit the default timeout so quadlet starts are delayed by 90s, nothing more.
There are plenty of workarounds documented here if that bothers you.

> > I switched to Debian from Fedora and this issue went away
> 
> Isn't redhat the backer of podman project? How come debian works better?

It's not Red Hat vs. Debian. I'm a Red Hat developer and I (also) maintain the [Debian package](https://tracker.debian.org/pkg/passt) providing pasta(1), if you can imagine such a thing.

That's the network back-end you're seemingly having problems with. It's not that Debian works better or worse, it's that the boot sequence is different. That might solve some symptoms and cause some others.

> If my understanding of the issue is right, any rootless podman container that needs outbound network connection would fail because of this bug.

That's of course not the case, these glitches only happen at boot depending on particular networking and system configurations. As @Luap99 explained the (robust) solution we're working on is in pasta(1) itself (let me also add https://bugs.passt.top/show_bug.cgi?id=141 and https://pad.passt.top/p/netlinkMonitor as references) and that's needed to properly fix the issue across distributions, including Debian and derivatives.

> And podman loves to talk about the feature that unlike docker, it can run rootlessly.

And it's not Podman vs. Docker either. Again, if you can imagine such a thing, I authored some bits of the rootlesskit integration for pasta which allow Docker to use it as well, see https://docs.docker.com/engine/security/rootless/troubleshoot/#networking-errors for an overview. You can run Docker rootlessly as well (perhaps not entirely, but this part definitely works).
